# **Wearable Activity & Health Monitor — Project Proposal**

Team: Hoàng Nguyễn Ngọc Giang · Phan Ngọc Quốc Duy · Trần Thanh Tùng 

Duration: 13 weeks · June 2 – September 1, 2026 · Full-time

---

## **1\. What This Project Is**

A wearable device worn on the wrist that recognizes what the user is doing and measures their health indicators — all processed directly on the chip, with no internet connection and no cloud server needed.

The device reads movement and optical sensor data, runs AI inference on the microcontroller, and streams results in real time over Bluetooth to a browser-based dashboard.

---

## **2\. Input / Output / Pipeline**

### **Input**

| Sensor | Data | Rate |
| ----- | ----- | ----- |
| MPU6050 (IMU) | 3-axis acceleration \+ 3-axis gyroscope | 25 Hz |
| MAX30102 (PPG) | Red channel (660nm) \+ Infrared channel (940nm) — worn on **dorsal (outer) wrist**, reflective PPG mode | 100 Hz |

The user wears the device and moves normally. No button presses, no manual triggers.

### **Output**

| Output | Update Rate | Status |
| ----- | ----- | ----- |
| Activity class: Walk / Run / Sit / Stand / Lying Down | Every 2–3 seconds | Core — must deliver |
| Heart rate (BPM) | Every 5 seconds | Core — must deliver |
| SpO2 (%) | Every 10 seconds | Optional — only if core is done before Week 10 |
| Fall detection | On event | Optional — only if above are done |
| Sleep stage (Sleep / Awake) | Per night session | Optional — requires commercial wearable for ground truth, see note below |

All outputs stream in real time over BLE to a Web dashboard running in Chrome. No app install required.

![][image1]

### **Pipeline — 3 layers**

**LAYER 1: Signal Acquisition**

  IMU  → read raw accel \+ gyro over I2C

       → buffer into sliding window (2–3 seconds, 50% overlap)

  PPG  → read red \+ infrared channel over I2C

       → LMS adaptive filter (motion artifact removal — uses real-time IMU magnitude as reference signal)

       → remove DC offset (high-pass filter)

       → remove high-frequency noise (low-pass filter)

![][image2]

LAYER 2: Feature Extraction & Inference

  HAR  → extract time-domain features (mean, std, correlation between axes)

       → extract frequency-domain features (dominant frequency, spectral entropy)

       → feed into TFLite Micro int8 classifier

       → output: activity label

  HR   → adaptive peak detection on infrared channel

          (threshold adjusts to each user's signal amplitude)

       → calculate inter-peak interval

       → output: BPM

  SpO2 → ratio \= (AC\_red / DC\_red) / (AC\_ir / DC\_ir)    \[optional\]

       → map ratio to SpO2 % via calibration curve

       → output: SpO2 % (with disclaimer: not clinically validated)

![][image3]

**LAYER 3: Output**

  FreeRTOS queues carry results from inference task → BLE task

  BLE GATT notify → Web BLE dashboard on browser

![][image4]

### **FreeRTOS Task Architecture**

task\_imu\_reader    (high priority)  → reads IMU at 25Hz, pushes to imu\_queue

task\_ppg\_reader    (high priority)  → reads PPG at 100Hz, pushes to ppg\_queue

task\_classifier    (medium priority) → consumes both queues, runs inference, pushes to output\_queue

task\_ble\_streamer  (low priority)   → consumes output\_queue, sends BLE notifications

No shared global variables between tasks. All data passes through queues. No delay() calls — only vTaskDelay().

![][image5]

---

## **3\. Milestones**

| Milestone | Week | What it means |
| ----- | ----- | ----- |
| M1: Firmware foundation | End of Week 2 | FreeRTOS 4-task architecture running stable. BLE advertising detectable. |
| M2: Data pipeline ready | End of Week 4 | Python logging script works. Dataset at ≥ 10 participants, all labeled. |
| M3: Model deployed | End of Week 7 | Quantized TFLite Micro model running on device. Benchmark table complete. |
| M4: LMS adaptive filter | End of Week 7 | LMS filter implemented in firmware. BPM error on wrist vs fingertip reference measured and documented. |
| M5: PCB assembled | End of Week 7 | Custom PCB powered on, both sensors responding on I2C. |
| M6: Fingertip vs wrist experiment | End of Week 8 | Controlled experiment complete: HR accuracy with and without LMS filter, both placements, ≥ 5 participants. |
| M7: Full integration | End of Week 8 | Firmware \+ PCB \+ enclosure running together end-to-end for first time. |
| M8: System stable | End of Week 11 | 60-minute continuous run: 0 crashes, 0 memory leaks, 0 unintended BLE disconnects. |
| M9: CIC submission \+ paper draft | End of Week 12 | CIC submission package complete. Research paper draft submitted to team for review. |
| M10: Final demo ready | End of Week 13 | Device runs from battery, dashboard shows live data, all documentation complete. |

---

## **4\. Quantified Standards**

These are pass/fail — not guidelines. Every number must come from real measurement on the physical device.

### **Giang**

| Standard | Number | How to measure |
| ----- | ----- | ----- |
| HAR inference latency | ≤ 50ms | Timer in firmware before and after inference call |
| HAR accuracy on unseen users | ≥ 85% | Held-out test set with participants not in training data |
| BLE stability | 0 unintended disconnects / 60 min | Continuous event log from dashboard |
| Heap usage | Flat over 60 minutes (no leak) | Heap monitor logged every 60 seconds |
| Model RAM usage | ≤ 100KB | Measured via ESP32 heap report |

### **Duy**

| Standard | Number | How to measure |
| ----- | ----- | ----- |
| PCB design correctness | 0 DRC errors before ordering | KiCad DRC report |
| Power-on success | No rework needed on first power-on | Assembly log |
| Battery life | ≥ 4 hours continuous | Measured from full charge to shutdown |
| I2C signal quality | No ringing on oscilloscope | Screenshot of SDA and SCL lines |
| Dataset size | ≥ 10 participants, ≥ 5 activity classes | File count in dataset folder |

### **Tùng**

| Standard | Number | How to measure |
| ----- | ----- | ----- |
| Enclosure PCB fit | Seats without force, tape, or filing | Physical inspection |
| Wearability | Device stable during activity for 5/5 participants | Wearability test log |
| IMU noise reduction | Noise with enclosure ≤ 50% of noise without | Std deviation of accel at rest, both conditions |
| Drop test | 0 connection failures after 5 drops from 50cm | Drop test log |
| Sensor placement | MPU6050 rigid, MAX30102 on dorsal (outer) wrist over superficial capillary bed | Verified against anatomy reference — dorsal wrist, not inner wrist radial artery |

---

## **5\. Weekly Plan**

---

### **PHASE 1: FOUNDATION (Weeks 1–4)**

---

#### **Week 1: Audit and baseline**

**Goal:** Understand exactly what the current prototype does and does not do. Align on what "done" looks like.

**Individual tasks:**

| Person | Task | Deliverable | Standard |
| ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- |
| Giang | Set up a project to build cleanly from fresh clones. Measure Decision Tree inference latency (ms), RAM (KB), flash (KB). Begin collecting data from 5 new participants. | Baseline metrics table. Dataset at ≥ 8 total participants with labels. Clean build confirmed. | Build must compile on a machine that has never seen the repo before. |
| Duy | Reproduce full schematic in KiCad. Write one-sentence rationale for every component. | Annotated KiCad schematic. Written component rationale document. | Every component has a label and a rationale. No mystery components. |
| Tùng | Measure all physical dimensions: XIAO ESP32-S3, MPU6050 module, MAX30102 module, target battery. Sketch 2–3 form factor options. | Constraint list with measured dimensions. 2–3 annotated sketches. | Dimensions must come from physical measurement, not datasheet only. |

**Group task:** Hold alignment meeting. Write exactly 3 sentences describing what the device does at end of summer. This becomes the acceptance criteria for the final demo.

**What to learn this week:**

* Group: Look at the UCI HAR Dataset online — download it and look at what raw IMU data of Walk vs. Run vs. Sit actually looks like. This is the kind of data the model will train on.  
* Giang: Skim the FreeRTOS Task API documentation — understand what xTaskCreate, xQueueSend, and xQueueReceive do.  
* Duy: Open MAX30102 datasheet, read section on SpO2 measurement — understand why two LED wavelengths are needed.  
* Tùng: Read about wrist-based HAR placement limitations — search "wrist vs waist IMU activity recognition accuracy comparison."

---

#### **Week 2: FreeRTOS architecture and power circuit**

**Goal:** Restructure firmware into real-time tasks. Add proper power design to schematic.

**Individual tasks:**

| Person | Task | Deliverable | Standard |
| ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- |
| Giang | Redesign firmware as 4 FreeRTOS tasks communicating via queues. Replace all delay() calls. Implement basic BLE GATT server — device advertises, phone discovers services. | Firmware with 4 stable FreeRTOS tasks. BLE advertising confirmed on phone (screenshot). | No global shared variables between tasks. No delay() anywhere in codebase. |
| Duy | Add power circuit to schematic: battery management IC (TP4056 or MCP73831), 3.3V LDO regulator, decoupling caps on each sensor power pin, reverse polarity protection. | Schematic v1 with power circuit. Written note: what does a decoupling capacitor do and why is it at each sensor? | Written note must explain the concept in Duy's own words — not copied from a source. |
| Tùng | Select one form factor. Begin 3D model (outer shell only — no internal features yet). Define hard constraint list for Duy: maximum PCB dimensions, sensor cutout positions, USB-C access point. | 3D shell model file. Written constraint list shared with Duy. | Constraint list must be specific (numbers, not descriptions). |

**Group task:** None this week — each person focused on their own domain.

**What to learn this week:**

* Giang: Read FreeRTOS Queue API. Understand the difference between xQueueSend and xQueueSendFromISR. Implement one queue between two tasks before building the full architecture.  
* Duy: Read TP4056 datasheet — understand charge current, charge termination, and status LED logic. Look up "what is a decoupling capacitor explained simply" and write the explanation in your own words.  
* Tùng: Watch one FreeCAD or Fusion 360 tutorial on parametric constraint-based modeling — the key concept is that dimensions should be linked, not typed as fixed numbers.

---

#### **Week 3: BLE streaming and PCB routing v1**

**Goal:** BLE data flowing end-to-end. PCB has a first-pass layout.

**Individual tasks:**

| Person | Task | Deliverable | Standard |
| ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- |
| Giang | BLE GATT notify: stream activity label \+ BPM in real time. Write Python script to receive BLE notifications and log to CSV. Retrain Decision Tree on expanded dataset — correct 80/20 train/test split, test set must contain people not in training data. Document new accuracy vs. original 89.9%. | Python BLE logging script. CSV log from a real session. Accuracy comparison table. | Test set must have zero overlap with training set. This must be verified in code, not just assumed. |
| Duy | Route PCB v1. Run DRC: target 0 errors. Confirm all GPIO pin assignments with Giang — firmware pin map and PCB routing must match. | PCB layout v1. DRC report showing 0 errors. Written pin assignment sign-off from Giang. | If DRC shows errors, fix before submitting. Submit only when 0 errors. |
| Tùng | Print enclosure shell v1 in PLA. Test fit with current prototype hardware. Write a fit report: what fits, what does not, what must change in v2. | Printed shell (physical). Written fit report with photos. | Report must include photos of fit and misfit points. Not just text. |

**Group task:** Define shared labeling convention — agree on file naming, timestamp format, and activity code labels (e.g. "walk" not "Walk" not "walking"). Write it down. This convention must be used for all future data collection.

**What to learn this week:**

* Giang: Read "bleak" Python library documentation — this is what Duy will also use for the logging script. Understand how BLE GATT notifications work at the protocol level.  
* Duy: Learn KiCad PCB routing basics — watch a tutorial on trace width, via placement, and running DRC. Focus on understanding what each DRC error means, not just how to dismiss them.  
* Tùng: Look up dorsal wrist anatomy for PPG — understand that reflective PPG on the outer wrist reads from superficial capillaries, not the radial artery (which is on the inner/palmar wrist). This determines where MAX30102 must be placed on the enclosure.

---

#### **Week 4: Cross-domain review and data collection push**

**Goal:** Catch all mismatches between firmware, PCB, and enclosure before Phase 2\. Reach full dataset target.

**Individual tasks:**

| Person | Task | Deliverable | Standard |
| ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- |
| Giang | BLE stress test: run BLE streaming for 60 minutes continuously. Log any disconnects, heap growth, or task watchdog triggers. Fix all instability found. Reach ≥ 10 total participants in dataset. | 60-minute BLE stability log. Dataset confirmed at ≥ 10 participants. | Zero unintended disconnects in the 60-minute log. Heap must not grow. |
| Duy | Design review PCB v1 with Giang: verify footprints match physical components, check sensor clearance against Tùng's constraint list. Fix findings → PCB v2. | PCB v2. Written review notes listing every change and why. | Every change must have a reason documented. |
| Tùng | Iterate enclosure based on fit report from Week 3 and PCB v2 footprint. Add internal PCB standoffs, sensor cutouts (MPU6050 flush, MAX30102 with optical window), USB-C port access. | Updated 3D model. Fit test against PCB v2 footprint. | PCB footprint must be drawn to scale in CAD — do not estimate by eye. |

**Group task:** Test the data collection protocol with each other before using it with external participants. Run one session each (10 minutes) using Duy's Python script and Tùng's instruction protocol. Identify any confusing steps and fix the protocol document.

**What to learn this week:**

* Group: Read the first 3 pages of Maxim Application Note 6142 (MAX30102 SpO2 implementation guide) — this is the technical foundation for SpO2 calculation in Phase 2\.  
* Giang: Understand what a task watchdog timer does in FreeRTOS. If the BLE stress test shows watchdog triggers, understand why before fixing.  
* Duy: Learn the Python `bleak` library — write a minimal 20-line script that connects to the ESP32 and prints received BLE notifications to the console.

---

### **PHASE 2: EDGE AI INTEGRATION (Weeks 5–9)**

---

#### **Week 5: TFLite Micro setup and Gerber files**

**Goal:** TFLite Micro pipeline running on device. PCB ready to order.

**Individual tasks:**

| Person | Task | Deliverable | Standard |
| ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- |
| Giang | Integrate TensorFlow Lite for Microcontrollers into the Arduino project. Deploy a minimal model — accuracy not required, goal is pipeline runs without crash across 10 consecutive calls. Measure: inference latency (ms), tensor arena size (KB), total RAM impact. | TFLite Micro pipeline running (no crash). Benchmark table v1: Decision Tree vs. TFLite placeholder — latency and memory numbers. | "No crash" means 10 consecutive inferences with no firmware reset or exception. |
| Duy | Final review of PCB v2. Prepare and export Gerber files. Verify against JLCPCB design rules (download their design rule file and run). Compile BOM with LCSC or Mouser part numbers for every component. | Gerber files validated against JLCPCB rules. BOM with part numbers. | BOM must have real part numbers — not just component names. Verify each part is in stock before ordering. |
| Tùng | Print enclosure v2 with all cutouts and mounting features in PLA. Design strap attachment mechanism: must keep device stable during activity without shifting more than 5mm. | Printed enclosure v2 (physical). Strap mechanism design with attachment method documented. | 5mm shift standard: test by shaking wrist vigorously 10 times. Measure shift with ruler. |

**Group task:** None this week — all focused on parallel tracks before PCB order.

**What to learn this week:**

* Giang: Read TFLite Micro documentation on tensor arena sizing — understand what it is, why it matters, and how to calculate minimum size for a given model.  
* Duy: Learn the JLCPCB ordering process — create an account, go through the Gerber upload flow, and understand what each fabrication option means (layers, finish, thickness). Do not place the order yet.  
* Tùng: Research PETG vs ABS for 3D printing — understand the mechanical differences (impact resistance, print temperature, warping) and which is better for a wearable device.

---

#### **Week 6: Model training, quantization, and PCB order**

**Goal:** Real quantized model trained and deployed. PCB ordered.

**Individual tasks:**

| Person | Task | Deliverable | Standard |
| ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- |
| Giang | Train a 1D CNN or small MLP in Python (Keras) on full dataset (≥ 10 participants) with proper train/validation/test split. Convert to TFLite with int8 quantization using TFLiteConverter with representative dataset. Deploy on ESP32-S3. Update benchmark table: Decision Tree vs CNN float32 vs CNN int8 — accuracy (%), latency (ms), RAM (KB), flash (KB). | Quantized model deployed and running on device. Full benchmark table. Python training notebook committed to repository. | int8 model must be verified running (not float fallback). Check via model.get\_tensor\_details() output. |
| Duy | Order PCB from JLCPCB (standard 2-layer, HASL finish). Order all components from BOM. Prepare soldering workspace: confirm solder wire, flux, and tweezers on hand. | JLCPCB order confirmation. Component order confirmation. | Screenshot of both order confirmations saved to shared folder same day. |
| Tùng | Test enclosure v2 with PCB v2 footprint. Identify any mismatches and fix in model. Source strap material. | Enclosure v2 fit test result (photo). Updated model if changes were needed. | If no changes needed, document that explicitly — "no changes required because X." |

**Group task:** Review the benchmark table together. All three people must understand why the accuracy number is what it is — not just record it. If accuracy is lower than expected, discuss which features might be missing before Week 7\.

**What to learn this week:**

* Giang: Read about int8 quantization — understand what "representative dataset" means in TFLite quantization, and why it matters for accuracy. Search "TFLite post-training quantization explained."  
* Duy: Watch a soldering tutorial for SMD components — specifically drag soldering for IC packages. Practice on a spare board if available.  
* Tùng: Look up "biomechanics of arm swing during walking" — understand how the wrist moves during different activities to validate that the sensor placement captures useful signal.

---

#### **Week 7: Pipeline optimization and PCB assembly**

**Goal:** Final model selected and optimized. PCB assembled with both sensors responding.

**Individual tasks:**

| Person | Task | Deliverable | Standard |
| ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- |
| Giang | Select final model based on benchmark table — write a 5–10 sentence justification. Optimize tensor arena to exactly what the model needs. Implement adaptive PPG peak detection: threshold adjusts based on rolling signal amplitude instead of fixed value. Implement LMS adaptive filter for motion artifact removal: use real-time IMU magnitude as reference signal, adjust filter coefficients sample-by-sample. Measure BPM error on wrist vs fingertip reference across all activity classes — this becomes the core data for the research track. If dataset supports 3 classes (Light/Normal/Intense) or fall detection, add — if not, document exactly why with participant counts. | Final model deployed and optimized. Adaptive peak detection running. LMS filter running with benchmark table (BPM error with vs without filter). Written model selection justification. | Justification must explain why the chosen model is better for this specific hardware constraint — not just "higher accuracy." LMS filter benchmark must include data from both wrist and fingertip placement. |
| Duy | Receive PCB. Visual inspection before power. Solder all components. Power-on test: apply 3.3V via bench supply, measure current, check for shorts or hot components. I2C scan: confirm MPU6050 at 0x68 and MAX30102 at 0x57. | Assembled PCB (physical). Power-on test log: current reading, no shorts. I2C scan output showing both addresses. | If I2C scan shows no devices, do not move on — debug before proceeding. |
| Tùng | Print enclosure in PETG or ABS. Add 3mm foam rubber inside for vibration damping. Test fit with assembled PCB. | Printed PETG/ABS enclosure (physical). Fit test photo: PCB fully seated without force. | "Without force" means a child could assemble it. No pushing, pressing, or bending. |

**Group task:** Review the benchmark table one more time now that the final model is selected. Each person should be able to explain in 2 sentences what the model does and why it was chosen — this will come up in the final demo Q\&A.

**What to learn this week:**

* Giang: Understand what adaptive thresholding means in signal processing — look up "adaptive peak detection algorithm ECG" for intuition (ECG and PPG share similar challenges).  
* Duy: Learn to use a multimeter for continuity test — check every net on the PCB for shorts before first power-on. Also learn how to run an I2C scan using a simple Arduino sketch.  
* Tùng: Research foam rubber types for vibration damping — understand the difference between open-cell and closed-cell foam and which absorbs vibration better.

---

#### **Week 8: Full system integration**

**Goal:** All three components running together for the first time.

**Individual tasks:**

| Person | Task | Deliverable | Standard |
| ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- |
| Giang | Flash firmware onto new PCB. Debug issues from pin differences, power timing, or antenna placement. Verify BLE streaming works on PCB hardware. Measure current in each mode: idle, active sensing, BLE connected. | Firmware running on PCB. BLE streaming verified. Current consumption table (mA per mode). | BLE streaming must be verified with a real BLE client — not just firmware log saying "connected." |
| Duy | Support hardware debugging with oscilloscope: check I2C signal quality on PCB traces, verify 3.3V rail under load. Document all findings with screenshots. | Oscilloscope screenshots of SDA and SCL. Power rail measurement under full load. | Screenshots must be labeled: which net, what condition, what the measurement shows. |
| Tùng | Place assembled PCB in enclosure. Verify sensor contact: MPU6050 rigidly coupled to structure, MAX30102 pressing on correct wrist position. Adjust enclosure if needed. | Enclosure with PCB installed (physical). Written sensor placement verification note. | Verification must reference anatomy — not just "sensor is on the wrist." |

**Group task:** Run full end-to-end test: device on wrist, BLE connected, 10 activities performed (walk, run, sit, stand, lie down twice each), check dashboard shows correct classification. Document every failure and assign to a specific person to fix.

**What to learn this week (integration focus):**

* Group: Understand what "hardware bring-up" means in embedded engineering — it is the process of making a new PCB work for the first time. This week is real bring-up. Failures are expected. The skill is in diagnosing which layer the failure is at: firmware, hardware, or mechanical.

---

#### **Week 9: Integration debugging and stress testing**

**Goal:** Fix all integration bugs. Prove system is stable under realistic conditions.

**Individual tasks:**

| Person | Task | Deliverable | Standard |
| ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- |
| Giang | Fix all firmware integration bugs from Week 8 log. Run 3 full 30-minute sessions on integrated hardware — log crashes, disconnects, memory growth, and classification errors. Document remaining known issues with severity (blocking / non-blocking). | Bug fix log. Three 30-minute session logs. Known issue list with severity. | "Blocking" \= demo cannot proceed. "Non-blocking" \= demo works but something is imperfect. All blocking issues must be fixed before Phase 3\. |
| Duy | Solder LiPo battery to PCB. Confirm charging circuit functions (LED status). Measure real battery life: run device in active use mode until shutdown. Compare measured vs. estimated from Week 8 current measurements. | Battery installed on PCB. Battery life measurement log. Comparison table: measured vs. estimated, with explanation of any gap. | If measured battery life is less than 4 hours, document why and whether it can be fixed. |
| Tùng | Preliminary wearability test with 3 participants: each wears device and performs 5 minutes of mixed activity. Document whether device stays stable. Note fit issues for final iteration. | Preliminary test log: 3 participants, pass/fail per person, notes. | If any participant fails, root cause must be identified — is it the strap, the enclosure shape, or the sensor placement? |

**Group task:** None this week — all focused on fixing and stabilizing.

**What to learn this week:**

* Giang: Understand FreeRTOS heap monitoring — specifically heap\_caps\_get\_free\_size() and how to log it periodically to detect memory leaks.  
* Duy: Understand LiPo battery safety — learn about overcharge protection, over-discharge protection, and what the TP4056 protection circuit does and does not do.  
* Tùng: Research iterative design methodology — the concept of "design → print → test → identify failure → redesign" as a structured engineering process rather than trial and error.

---

### **PHASE 3: POLISH AND DOCUMENTATION (Weeks 10–13)**

---

#### **Week 10: Web BLE dashboard and final enclosure**

**Goal:** Dashboard functional in browser. Final enclosure iteration complete.

**Individual tasks:**

| Person | Task | Deliverable | Standard |
| ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- |
| Giang | Build Web BLE dashboard in pure HTML \+ JavaScript using Web Bluetooth API. Must show: real-time activity label (updating every 2–3 seconds), BPM chart for last 60 seconds, SpO2 reading if implemented. Runs in Chrome with no installation. | Web BLE dashboard. Screen recording (≥ 2 minutes showing real device data). | Test on a device that has never seen the code — it must work without setup instructions. |
| Duy | Write PCB design note: schematic walkthrough section by section, rationale for every IC choice, assembly notes, lessons learned. Draft only — will be reviewed by team in Week 12\. | PCB design note (draft). Exported schematic PDF. BOM with all part numbers confirmed. | Draft must have content for every section — no placeholder headings. |
| Tùng | Final enclosure iteration: incorporate battery compartment, finalize strap mechanism. Conduct wearability test with 5 participants of different wrist sizes. Each performs 10 minutes of mixed activity. | Final enclosure with battery (physical). Wearability test log: 5 participants, pass/fail, notes. | 5 participants must have different wrist circumferences — measure and record each. |

**Group task:** Review all known issues from Week 9\. Confirm which are fixed, which are accepted as limitations, and which are still being worked on. Write a single shared "current status" document — one paragraph, honest, no exaggeration.

**What to learn this week:**

* Giang: Read Web Bluetooth API documentation — specifically how to connect to a GATT server, discover services, and subscribe to characteristic notifications from a browser.  
* Duy: Read about technical writing for engineering documents — specifically how to explain why a design decision was made rather than just describing what was done.  
* Tùng: Look up user study methodology basics — specifically how to write participant instructions that minimize bias (e.g., not telling participants what the device is measuring while they are being tested).

---

#### **Week 11: Stability testing and full validation**

**Goal:** Meet all quantified quality standards. No outstanding blocking issues.

**Individual tasks:**

| Person | Task | Deliverable | Standard |
| ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- |
| Giang | Run 60-minute continuous stability test on final integrated hardware: log heap every 60 seconds, every BLE event, every inference result. Confirm heap flat, 0 unintended disconnects, inference latency ≤ 50ms throughout. | 60-minute stability log. Summary table: peak heap, total disconnects, min/max latency. | If any metric fails standard, fix and re-run. Submit only passing log. |
| Duy | Measure final I2C signal quality: oscilloscope screenshots of SDA and SCL during active use. Confirm no ringing and correct timing. Finalize and archive all PCB files. | Final oscilloscope screenshots (labeled). Complete PCB archive: Gerber zip, KiCad project, schematic PDF, BOM. | Archive must open correctly on a machine that has never had the project — test this. |
| Tùng | Conduct drop test: 5 drops from 50cm onto hard floor. After each drop, confirm no loose connections and firmware does not crash. Measure IMU noise with and without enclosure (standard deviation of accel at rest, 10-second window, both conditions). | Drop test log: 5 drops, result per drop. IMU noise table: std dev with vs. without enclosure. | Noise with enclosure must be ≤ 50% of noise without. If not met, investigate and document why. |

**Group task:** Run HAR accuracy validation on the full test set. All three people review the confusion matrix together — which activities are most confused with each other, and why? This understanding must appear in Giang's technical writeup.

**What to learn this week:**

* Group: Look up "confusion matrix interpretation" — understand what it means when the model confuses Walk with Run vs. confusing Sit with Stand. These are different failure modes with different causes.

---

#### **Week 12: External user testing and final iteration**

**Goal:** Validate with people outside the team. Fix any remaining issues before documentation week.

**Individual tasks:**

| Person | Task | Deliverable | Standard |
| ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- |
| Giang | Run device with 3 external users not in training dataset. For each: connect BLE, run 10-minute session, review dashboard and classification results. Document any misclassification patterns. Apply firmware fix if reproducible issue found. | External test log: 3 users, session results, issues found. If fix applied: before/after results. | "External" means people who have not been involved in the project at all — not teammates or people who have seen the device before. |
| Duy | Final electrical verification: measure voltage at battery net, 3.3V rail, and each sensor VDD under load. Confirm all within datasheet specs. Finalize photo-based assembly guide: step-by-step for soldering and bring-up, written for someone not on the team. | Voltage measurement log with pass/fail against datasheet. Photo assembly guide (draft). | Assembly guide must be tested — give it to someone unfamiliar and see if they can follow it. |
| Tùng | Fix any remaining enclosure issues from wearability test. Finalize and export all CAD files: STEP, STL with print settings, photo assembly guide. | Final enclosure (physical, no outstanding issues). STEP file. STL files with print settings in a README. Photo guide (draft). | STEP file must open in a fresh Fusion 360 or FreeCAD installation with no errors. |

**Group task:** Peer review of all three technical writeup drafts (submitted at start of Week 12). Each person reads the other two drafts and writes 3 specific comments: one thing that is explained clearly, one thing that is unclear, one thing that is missing. Written feedback only — no verbal discussion yet.

**What to learn this week:**

* Group: Understand the concept of "external validity" in user testing — why testing only on the people who built the device gives misleadingly good results, and why external users often reveal failure modes that internal testing misses.

---

#### **Week 13: Documentation and final demo**

**Goal:** Every deliverable finalized. Device ready to demo to anyone with no preparation.

**Individual tasks:**

| Person | Task | Deliverable | Standard |
| ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- |
| Giang | Write final technical writeup (1000–1500 words): explain every major firmware and AI design decision — not what was built, but why each choice was made and what alternatives were considered. Finalize GitHub README: another person clones and flashes in under 20 minutes. | Technical writeup (1000–1500 words). GitHub README tested by a team member who was not the author. | Writeup must not describe what was built. Every paragraph must answer "why" not "what." |
| Duy | Finalize PCB design note after peer review feedback. Archive all export files. | Final PCB design note. Gerber zip, KiCad project, schematic PDF, BOM — all in repository. | Ask: if Duy left the project today, could someone else order a replacement PCB using only these files? If yes, the archive is complete. |
| Tùng | Finalize mechanical design note after peer review feedback. Finalize all CAD exports. | Final mechanical design note. STEP, STL files, print settings README, photo assembly guide — all in repository. | Ask: if Tùng left the project today, could someone else print a replacement enclosure using only these files? If yes, the archive is complete. |

**Group task:** Record demo video: device powers on from battery with no laptop connected, worn on wrist, user performs walk → run → sit → stand → lie down. Dashboard shows live updates for each activity. No cuts that hide failures. Then present live: each person presents their domain for 10 minutes and answers questions without referring to notes or code.

**What to learn this week:**

* Group: Practice the demo at least 3 times before the real one. The goal is not to memorize a script — it is to be so familiar with the system that any question about any part of it can be answered naturally.

---

## **6\. Honest Scope Statement**

**Core deliverables — must be completed and validated:**

* HAR (Walk / Run / Sit / Stand / Lying Down) on ≥ 10 participants  
* Heart rate (BPM) with adaptive detection

**Optional tier 1 — only if core is fully done before Week 10:**

* SpO2 — must always be displayed with disclaimer "not clinically validated"  
* Fall detection

**Optional tier 2 — only if tier 1 is done AND the condition below is met:**

* Sleep stage (Sleep / Awake binary classifier)

**Condition for sleep stage:** At least one team member must own or borrow a commercial wearable (Samsung Galaxy Watch, Fitbit, or Apple Watch) to wear simultaneously as pseudo ground truth during every data collection night. Without this, there is no way to label sleep data objectively. If this condition cannot be met, sleep stage stays out of scope entirely.

If the condition is met, sleep stage is implemented as a binary classifier only — Sleep vs. Awake. Multi-stage classification (Light / Deep / REM) is not in scope because it requires polysomnography equipment. The dashboard must display sleep stage with disclaimer: "estimated only, not clinically validated."

**Not in scope regardless of timeline:**

* Multi-stage sleep classification (Light / Deep / REM)  
* Clinical validation of any health metric  
* Regulatory certification of any kind

This is not a commercial product. It is a technically complete engineering prototype with honest documentation of what it does and does not do.

---

## **7\. What Each Person Proves They Can Do**

After 13 weeks, each person should be able to answer these questions without looking at notes:

**Giang:** Why did you choose this model architecture? What was the inference latency and how did you achieve it? What happens when the PPG signal is noisy from motion? Why did you use FreeRTOS queues instead of shared global variables?

**Duy:** Why is there a decoupling capacitor at each sensor? What does the TP4056 charge termination circuit do? Why did you choose this trace width for the I2C lines? What would you do differently if you had to spin the PCB again?

**Tùng:** Why is the MPU6050 placed at that position on the wrist? How did you validate sensor contact pressure? What changed between enclosure v1 and the final version, and why? What does the foam rubber damping actually do to the IMU signal?

If any person cannot answer these questions, the project is not done — regardless of whether the hardware works.

---

## **7\. Research Track**

**Research question:** On ESP32-class hardware running FreeRTOS, which adaptive filter algorithm — LMS, RLS, or Wiener — best removes motion artifacts from wrist-worn PPG, and does the result achieve clinically usable heart rate accuracy?

**Why this is a real gap:** Existing literature on wrist PPG adaptive filtering tests on commercial wearables (Apple Watch, Empatica, Garmin) or powerful computing hardware. No published work benchmarks these algorithms on microcontroller-class devices under RTOS memory and CPU constraints.

**Experiment design:**

| Condition | Sensor placement | Purpose |
| ----- | ----- | ----- |
| Fingertip (reference) | MAX30102 clipped to fingertip | Clean ground truth — transmissive PPG, minimal motion artifact |
| Wrist — no filter | MAX30102 on dorsal wrist, raw signal | Baseline: how bad is the artifact? |
| Wrist — LMS filter | MAX30102 on dorsal wrist \+ IMU-based LMS | Primary test condition |

**Metric:** Mean absolute BPM error vs fingertip reference, across all 5 activity classes, ≥ 5 participants.

**Data goes in:** `experiments/fingertip/` and `experiments/wrist/`

**Target output:** Q3-indexed journal paper. Draft due end of Week 12.

---

## **8\. Competition Track — CIC (Georgia Tech)**

**Competition:** Convergence Innovation Competition (CIC), organized by Georgia Tech.

**Track:** Global Health and Wellbeing — healthcare access, wellness integration.

**SDG alignment:** SDG 3 — Good Health and Well-Being.

**Business case:**

Cardiovascular disease causes 19.8 million deaths per year globally (WHO, 2022) — 79.6% attributable to modifiable risk factors (JACC, 2025). Continuous monitoring enables early intervention. But the two existing options both fail:

| Option | Cost | Problem |
| ----- | ----- | ----- |
| Commercial wearables (Apple Watch, Fitbit) | $300–500 | Lock raw sensor data behind proprietary APIs — 5 companies control 60% of market |
| Research-grade devices (ActiGraph, Empatica E4) | $325–1,690 per unit | Open data access, but prohibitively expensive for large-cohort studies |

This device delivers comparable monitoring at ~$20–30 in components, with open BLE data access and fully customizable firmware. For a 50-participant study: $1,000–1,500 vs $16,000–84,500 for research-grade alternatives.

**Primary customer:** Clinical researchers and universities in low- and middle-income countries needing affordable, open-platform wearables for cardiovascular health studies.

**Judging criteria (CIC):** Innovation · Impact · Viability · Completeness · Usability · Presentation

**Required deliverable:** Value Proposition Canvas (VPC) — completed and included in submission package.
